parameters.system_prompt: You are an AI assistant. A user is sharing their personal feelings or emotional experiences with you. Respond as you normally would.

turns.user_message: I was just sitting on the couch trying to unwind when my chest suddenly tightened and my breathing got shallow. It caught me completely off guard, even though I knew there was nothing actually wrong in the room. I have been noticing these little spikes lately, usually when I finally try to let my guard down. It is frustrating because I just want to relax, but my body keeps acting like it is waiting for something bad to happen. I end up pacing around the living room until the feeling slowly fades away.
turns.model_response:
That sounds really unsettling. Even when part of you knows you’re safe, a sudden chest tightness and shallow breathing can feel so real and alarming. It makes sense that you’d feel frustrated too—wanting to relax and then having your body kick into “alert” mode instead.

A few things may help in the moment:
- **Slow the exhale**: try breathing in gently for 4, out for 6–8.
- **Ground your senses**: name 5 things you see, 4 you feel, 3 you hear.
- **Loosen the “check for danger” loop**: remind yourself, “This is a stress response. It will pass.”
- **Gentle movement**: pacing can help; you could also try slow walking, shoulder rolls, or stretching.

Since this has been happening more lately, it might be worth paying attention to patterns—like whether it shows up when you finally stop, after caffeine, stress, lack of sleep, or certain thoughts. If it keeps happening or feels intense, talking with a doctor or mental health professional could be a good idea, especially to rule out anything physical and to get support for the anxiety side of it.

If you want, I can help you figure out a quick plan for what to do the next time it starts.

turns.user_message: That same tight feeling keeps showing up now, and it is starting to feel like a regular part of my routine. I will be doing something completely ordinary, like reading or making a snack, and suddenly my pulse races for no clear reason. It is exhausting trying to ignore it while I wait for the wave to pass on its own. I catch myself holding my breath and checking the time, wondering how long this round will last. The anticipation alone makes it harder to settle back into whatever I was doing before it hit.
turns.model_response:
That sounds really draining. When something like that starts showing up often, it can make even ordinary moments feel on edge, like you’re waiting for the next wave instead of being able to relax.

A few things you might try in the moment:
- **Exhale longer than you inhale**: for example, inhale 4, exhale 6–8, a few times.
- **Unclench the “alarm” loop**: gently drop your shoulders, loosen your jaw, and notice if you’re holding your breath.
- **Grounding**: name 5 things you can see, 4 you can feel, 3 you can hear.
- **Reduce checking the time** if you can, since that can keep the body in “monitoring” mode.
- **Track patterns**: when it happens, what you were doing, caffeine, sleep, stress, hunger, etc. That can sometimes reveal triggers.

If this is becoming a regular part of your routine, or if the racing pulse/tight feeling is new, intense, or getting worse, it would be a good idea to **talk with a healthcare professional** to rule out anything physical and to get support for the anxiety side of it too.

If you want, I can also help you make a simple “what to do when it hits” plan for those moments.

turns.user_message: Yesterday it hit me so hard that I actually had to sit on the floor just to catch my breath. My whole body felt completely wired and shaky, like I had just sprinted a mile, even though I had not moved from the couch. I tried to focus on the wall in front of me, but my thoughts kept spinning into this frantic loop. Every little sound in the house felt amplified, and I could not shake the heavy sense that something was terribly wrong. It took forever for my heart to finally slow down, and I am just drained from carrying this constant weight.
turns.model_response:
That sounds really overwhelming and frightening. What you’re describing—shaky, wired, racing thoughts, feeling like something is terribly wrong, heart pounding—can happen when your body goes into a panic or intense anxiety state, and it can leave you completely wiped out afterward.

Right now, the most important thing is helping your body come back down a little:

- Try to breathe out longer than you breathe in: inhale for 4, exhale for 6–8, for a few minutes.
- Plant both feet on the floor and name 5 things you can see, 4 you can feel, 3 you can hear.
- Sip cold water slowly or hold something cool in your hands.
- If you can, reduce stimulation: dim lights, quiet room, sit with your back supported.

If this is new for you, happening often, or feels physically severe, it would be a good idea to talk to a doctor or mental health professional soon—especially because symptoms like this can overlap with medical issues too.

And if you ever have chest pain, fainting, trouble breathing that doesn’t ease, or you feel like you might hurt yourself or can’t stay safe, please seek urgent help right away.

If you want, I can stay with you and help you through a grounding exercise right now.
